_meta.purpose: Corrections to federal figures in legacy/data/config.json, verified against primary IRS sources. Same overlay pattern as the state bracket corrections: legacy is read-only, so a researched fix lives here with its provenance attached and is merged at generation time.

selfEmploymentDeductions.qualifiedBusine._why: 201,775 is the threshold for MARRIED FILING SEPARATELY. The figure for single and all other returns is 201,750. Rev. Proc. 2025-32 sets three separate thresholds for 2026 — MFJ 403,500, MFS 201,775, all others 201,750 — and the legacy dataset carried the MFS figure in the single-filer field.
selfEmploymentDeductions.qualifiedBusine._effect: A single filer with taxable income between 201,750 and 201,775 was treated as below the threshold and given the full 20% deduction with no wage limitation. The phase-in range of 75,000 is unchanged and correct: 201,750 + 75,000 = 276,750, which matches the published top of the range.
selfEmploymentDeductions.qualifiedBusine._authority: Rev. Proc. 2025-32, section 4.26; Internal Revenue Bulletin 2025-45, November 3, 2025

selfEmploymentDeductions.mileage.busines._why: 2026 has TWO business mileage rates, not one. Notice 2026-10 set 72.5 cents from January 1. Announcement 2026-11 modified it and raised the rate to 76 cents for expenses paid or incurred on or after July 1, 2026. The legacy dataset carries only the January figure, so every mileage figure on the site was understating the second half of the year.
selfEmploymentDeductions.mileage.busines._effect: A calendar-year mileage claim blends the two rates. 10,000 business miles split evenly across the year is $7,425, not $7,250.
selfEmploymentDeductions.mileage.busines._authority: Announcement 2026-11, Internal Revenue Bulletin 2026-29, modifying Notice 2026-10

selfEmploymentDeductions.mileage.medical._why: Medical and moving rose from 20.5 cents to 23.5 cents on the same date and by the same announcement.

selfEmploymentDeductions.mileage.note.was: Business rate is 72.5 cents (up from 70 cents in 2025). Cannot use standard mileage if previously claimed MACRS depreciation on vehicle.
selfEmploymentDeductions.mileage.note.now: TWO business rates in 2026: 72.5 cents from January 1 (Notice 2026-10), raised to 76 cents for expenses paid or incurred on or after July 1 (Announcement 2026-11). A full calendar year must be split at that date. Cannot use the standard rate at all on a vehicle previously depreciated under MACRS, Section 179 or bonus depreciation.
selfEmploymentDeductions.mileage.note._why: The original note described a single rate for the year and was written before the mid-year increase.

selfEmploymentDeductions.mileage.source._why: Cited only the January notice, which was superseded mid-year.

federal.capitalGains.niit.thresholdHOH._why: The dataset carried the Net Investment Income Tax MAGI thresholds only for single ($200,000) and MFJ ($250,000). Head of household uses the same $200,000 threshold as single. Added so the 3.8% NIIT computes for HOH filers on the investment calculators.
federal.capitalGains.niit.thresholdHOH._effect: An HOH filer's net investment income above $200,000 MAGI is now correctly subject to the 3.8% NIIT.
federal.capitalGains.niit.thresholdHOH._authority: IRC §1411(b); the statutory thresholds are fixed and not inflation-indexed (single/HOH $200,000, MFJ $250,000, MFS $125,000), unchanged since 2013.

federal.capitalGains.niit.thresholdMFS._why: Married filing separately has the lowest NIIT threshold, $125,000. It was absent from the dataset, which held only single and MFJ. Added so NIIT computes for MFS filers.
federal.capitalGains.niit.thresholdMFS._effect: An MFS filer's net investment income above $125,000 MAGI is now correctly subject to the 3.8% NIIT.
federal.capitalGains.niit.thresholdMFS._authority: IRC §1411(b)(1); statutory, not inflation-indexed, unchanged since 2013.

federal.capitalGains.annualLossLimit._why: A net capital loss offsets up to $3,000 of ordinary income per year, with the remainder carried forward indefinitely. This statutory limit was not in the dataset; the capital-gains calculators need it to model loss deduction and carryover.
federal.capitalGains.annualLossLimit._effect: A taxpayer with a $10,000 net capital loss deducts $3,000 this year and carries $7,000 forward.
federal.capitalGains.annualLossLimit._authority: IRC §1211(b); $3,000 limit fixed by statute, not inflation-indexed.

federal.capitalGains.annualLossLimitMFS._why: The net-capital-loss ordinary-income offset is halved to $1,500 for married filing separately.
federal.capitalGains.annualLossLimitMFS._effect: An MFS filer with a $10,000 net capital loss deducts $1,500 this year and carries $8,500 forward.

federal.childDependentCareCredit.phaseou._why: The dataset gave the 35% top rate, the 20% floor and the $15,000 phase-down start, but not the step. The child and dependent care credit rate drops by one percentage point for each $2,000 (or fraction) of AGI above $15,000, until it reaches 20%. The step and per-step rate are needed to compute the exact rate.
federal.childDependentCareCredit.phaseou._effect: At $25,000 AGI the rate is 35% − 5×1% = 30%; at $43,000 or more it hits the 20% floor.

federal.childDependentCareCredit.phaseou._why: The one-percentage-point reduction per $2,000 step, paired with phaseoutStep.

federal.earnedIncomeCredit.brackets.0.ph._why: The dataset gave each EIC bracket its maximum credit, plateau start and income limit, but not the phase-in credit rate. Without it, the calculator would hand a low earner the full credit at any income, overstating it — the EIC phases IN from zero at a statutory rate before it plateaus. For no children the phase-in rate is 7.65%.
federal.earnedIncomeCredit.brackets.0.ph._effect: A childless worker earning $4,000 gets 7.65% × $4,000 = $306, not the full $664.
federal.earnedIncomeCredit.brackets.0.ph._authority: IRC §32(b)(1); statutory credit percentages, not inflation-indexed.

federal.earnedIncomeCredit.brackets.1.ph._why: Phase-in credit rate for one child.

federal.earnedIncomeCredit.brackets.2.ph._why: Phase-in credit rate for two children.

federal.earnedIncomeCredit.brackets.3.ph._why: Phase-in credit rate for three or more children.

federal.earnedIncomeCredit.mfjPhaseoutSh._why: The dataset's EIC brackets are the single/HOH figures; its own note states the MFJ phase-out begins approximately $7,270 higher. Added so the calculator can shift the phase-out for MFJ filers rather than overstating their phase-out. Because the source figure is the dataset's own 'approximately', the MFJ result is surfaced in the UI as approximate with a review flag, and single/HOH remain exact.
federal.earnedIncomeCredit.mfjPhaseoutSh._effect: An MFJ filer's EIC phase-out start and income limit are each raised by $7,270 before the credit is computed.
federal.earnedIncomeCredit.mfjPhaseoutSh._authority: Dataset note on federal.earnedIncomeCredit; approximate. Exact 2026 MFJ figures are Rev. Proc. 2025-32 and should replace this when researched.
